title: Asymptotic properties of stochastic semilinear equations by the method of lower measures
overall_summary: The paper develops and applies the method of lower measures, also called the l-condition, to prove total variation convergence of Markov evolution systems and, in the time-homogeneous case, uniqueness and mixing of invariant measures. The abstract mechanism is: prove that all sufficiently late transition laws asymptotically dominate a common nonzero finite measure, then total variation distances between any two evolved probability measures vanish. The paper then verifies this mechanism for Hilbert-space semilinear stochastic equations with additive cylindrical noise by combining recurrence into a ball, a one-step minorization on that ball, Girsanov comparison with an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, and a lower bound on Gaussian transition densities. For the original SPDE with singular Œ¥0 drift, the most directly portable content is the abstract lower-measure criterion and the recurrence-plus-minorization template; the Hilbert-space density estimates suggest a possible route through smooth drift approximations and uniform one-step lower bounds, although the paper itself treats regular H-valued drifts satisfying restrictive summability and exponential-integrability assumptions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.4 (lower measures imply merging and, in the homogeneous case, invariant mixing)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,F) be a measurable space. Let M be the finite signed measures on F, M_+ the finite nonnegative measures, P the probability measures, and ‚à•ŒΩ‚à• the total variation norm of a signed measure ŒΩ, with ŒΩ^- its negative variation. A Markov operator is a linear map S:M‚ÜíM such that S(P)‚äÇP. Let Œ£={S_{s,t}:0‚â§s‚â§t<‚àû} be an evolution system of Markov operators, meaning S_{u,t}S_{s,u}=S_{s,t} for 0‚â§s‚â§u‚â§t. A family {Œº_s:s‚â•0}‚äÇM_+ is a system of lower measures for Œ£ if inf_{s‚â•0} Œº_s(X)>0 and, for every fixed s‚â•0 and every ŒΩ‚ààP, ‚à•(S_{s,t}ŒΩ‚àíŒº_s)^-‚à•‚Üí0 as t‚Üí‚àû. If such a system of lower measures exists, then for every s‚â•0 and every ŒΩ_1,ŒΩ_2‚ààP one has ‚à•S_{s,t}ŒΩ_1‚àíS_{s,t}ŒΩ_2‚à•‚Üí0 as t‚Üí‚àû. If Œ£ is homogeneous, meaning S_{s,t}=S_{s+h,t+h} for all h‚â•0 and S_t:=S_{0,t}, then existence of a lower measure is equivalent to existence of an invariant probability measure Œº* with S_t Œº*=Œº* for all t‚â•0 and ‚à•S_tŒΩ‚àíŒº*‚à•‚Üí0 as t‚Üí‚àû for every ŒΩ‚ààP.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Maslowski [27], based on Lasota-Yorke type lower-measure arguments, rather than reproving it. The underlying mechanism is that the l-condition gives a common nonzero asymptotic submeasure in all evolved laws S_{s,t}ŒΩ. Removing the common part from two evolved probability measures yields a strict loss of total variation mass, and iteration forces the distance between any two evolved probabilities to vanish. In the homogeneous case, convergence of S_tŒΩ for all ŒΩ gives an invariant limit by the semigroup property; conversely, if an invariant probability attracts all initial laws in total variation then it itself supplies a lower measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5 (quantitative lower-measure contraction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Œ£={S_{s,t}:0‚â§s‚â§t<‚àû} be an evolution system of Markov operators on a measurable space (X,F). Let {Œº_s:s‚â•0}‚äÇM_+ be finite nonnegative measures with Œª:=inf_{s‚â•0} Œº_s(X)>0. Fix ŒΩ_1,ŒΩ_2‚ààP, Œµ‚àà[0,1/2], and Œ±‚àà(0,1/2). Assume there is a set B‚ààF such that (i) S_{œÉ,t}ŒΩ_1(X‚àñB)‚â§Œµ and S_{œÉ,t}ŒΩ_2(X‚àñB)‚â§Œµ for every 0‚â§œÉ‚â§t<‚àû, and (ii) there exists œÑ‚â•0 such that for every probability Œº with Œº(B)=1 and every œÉ‚â•0, ‚à•(S_{œÉ,œÉ+œÑ}Œº‚àíŒº_œÉ)^-‚à•<ŒªŒ±. Put q_Œ±:=1‚àí(1‚àí2Œ±)Œª, so q_Œ±‚àà(0,1). Then for every s‚â•0, every n‚ààN, and every t‚â•s+nœÑ, ‚à•S_{s,t}ŒΩ_1‚àíS_{s,t}ŒΩ_2‚à•‚â§q_Œ±^n‚à•ŒΩ_1‚àíŒΩ_2‚à•+12Œµ‚àë_{i=0}^n q_Œ±^i‚â§q_Œ±^n‚à•ŒΩ_1‚àíŒΩ_2‚à•+12Œµ/(1‚àíq_Œ±).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is an induction on n. Suppose the estimate has been obtained up to time s+nœÑ. Let ŒΩ_i^n:=S_{s,s+nœÑ}ŒΩ_i. Normalize the restrictions of ŒΩ_i^n to B by Œº_i(A)=c_iŒΩ_i^n(A‚à©B), where c_i=ŒΩ_i^n(B)^{-1}. Assumption (i) and Œµ‚â§1/2 give ‚à•ŒΩ_i^n‚àíŒº_i‚à•‚â§2(Œµ+Œµ/(1‚àíŒµ))‚â§6Œµ. Let Œº:=Œº_1‚àíŒº_2 and Œ∑:=‚à•Œº^+‚à•=‚à•Œº^-‚à•=‚à•Œº‚à•/2. If Œ∑>0, then Œ∑^{-1}Œº^+ and Œ∑^{-1}Œº^- are probabilities supported on B. Assumption (ii) gives ‚à•(S_{s+nœÑ,s+(n+1)œÑ}Œ∑^{-1}Œº^¬±‚àíŒº_{s+nœÑ})^-‚à•<ŒªŒ±. Since each SŒ∑^{-1}Œº^¬± has total mass 1 and Œº_{s+nœÑ}(X)‚â•Œª, the total variation identity ‚à•œÅ‚àím‚à•=œÅ(X)‚àím(X)+2‚à•(œÅ‚àím)^-‚à• for nonnegative measures yields ‚à•SŒ∑^{-1}Œº^¬±‚àíŒº_{s+nœÑ}‚à•‚â§1‚àíŒª+2ŒªŒ±=q_Œ±. Therefore ‚à•S_{s+nœÑ,s+(n+1)œÑ}Œº‚à•‚â§q_Œ±‚à•Œº‚à•. Combining this contraction with the two 6Œµ errors from replacing ŒΩ_i^n by Œº_i and using the induction hypothesis gives the next-step bound. Finally, every Markov operator has total variation operator norm at most one on signed measures, so the estimate at s+nœÑ extends to all later t‚â•s+nœÑ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.7 (recurrence plus one-step minorization gives lower measures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P(s,x,t,A), 0‚â§s‚â§t<‚àû, x‚ààX, A‚ààF, be the transition function of a nonhomogeneous Markov process on a measurable space (X,F). Define the adjoint Markov evolution system P^*_{s,t}ŒΩ(A):=‚à´_X P(s,x,t,A)ŒΩ(dx). Assume that for every s‚â•0 there exist Œ≤(s)>0, a finite nonnegative measure Œºhat_s‚ààM_+, and a measurable set B(s)‚ààF such that (i) for every x‚ààX there is t_0=t_0(s,x) with P(s,x,t,B(s))‚â•Œ≤(s) for all t‚â•t_0, (ii) for every A‚ààF, inf{P(t,x,t+1,A):x‚ààB(s), t‚â•s}‚â•Œºhat_s(A), and (iii) inf_{s‚â•0} Œ≤(s)Œºhat_s(X)>0. Then {Œ≤(s)Œºhat_s:s‚â•0} is a system of lower measures for {P^*_{s,t}}. If Œ≤, B, and Œºhat can be chosen independent of s, then Œ≤ Œºhat is a lower measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For t‚â•s and A‚ààF, use the Chapman-Kolmogorov formula: P^*_{s,t+1}ŒΩ(A)=‚à´_X‚à´_X P(t,y,t+1,A)P(s,x,t,dy)ŒΩ(dx). Restrict the inner integral to y‚ààB(s) and apply the one-step lower bound (ii) to get P^*_{s,t+1}ŒΩ(A)‚â•Œºhat_s(A)‚à´_X P(s,x,t,B(s))ŒΩ(dx). By (i) and Fatou's lemma, the liminf of the scalar factor is at least Œ≤(s) for each initial probability ŒΩ. Hence P^*_{s,t+1}ŒΩ asymptotically dominates Œ≤(s)Œºhat_s in the sense that ‚à•(P^*_{s,t+1}ŒΩ‚àíŒ≤(s)Œºhat_s)^-‚à•‚Üí0. Condition (iii) gives the uniform positive mass required in the definition of a system of lower measures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conditioned Ornstein-Uhlenbeck bridge representation (equations 2.7-2.9)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting of Section 2, H has an orthonormal eigenbasis {e_n} for a negative self-adjoint operator A with Ae_n=‚àíŒª_ne_n, Œª_n>0. Let W be a cylindrical Wiener process with scalar components w_n(t)=‚ü®W_t,e_n‚ü©. The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck equation is dZ_r=AZ_rdr+dW_r. For fixed t‚â•0 and endpoints x,y‚ààH, write x_n=‚ü®x,e_n‚ü© and y_n=‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©. The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process conditioned to go from x at time t to y at time t+1, denoted Zhat(s)=Z^{t,x}_{t+1,y}(s), s‚àà[t,t+1], is represented by Zhat(s)=‚àë_{n‚â•1} Zhat_n(s)e_n, where Y_n(s):=((1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)})/e^{‚àíŒª_n(t+1‚àís)})‚à´_t^s e^{‚àíŒª_n(t+1‚àíu)}(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àíu)})^{-1}dw_n(u), and Zhat_n(s)=e^{‚àíŒª_n(s‚àít)}(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)})(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n})^{-1}x_n+e^{Œª_n(t+1‚àís)}(e^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)}‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n})(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n})^{-1}y_n+Y_n(s).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls this formula from Sim√£o [34]. Later, in the proof of Lemma 3.2, the same bridge is characterized by the scalar stochastic differential equation dZhat_n(s)=dw_n(s)‚àíŒª_nZhat_n(s)ds‚àí2Œª_n(e^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)}Zhat_n(s)‚àíe^{‚àíŒª_n(t+1‚àís)}y_n)(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)})^{-1}ds with Zhat_n(t)=x_n. Solving this linear equation gives the displayed formula and ensures Zhat(t)=x and Zhat(t+1)=y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2 (uniform lower bound on nonlinear transition density relative to OU)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space, let A be self-adjoint, negative, and have an orthonormal eigenbasis {e_n} with Ae_n=‚àíŒª_ne_n, Œª_n‚â•Œª_0>0, and ‚àë_n Œª_n^{-1}<‚àû. Let W be cylindrical Brownian motion on H. Let X solve dX_r=[AX_r+f(r,X_r)]dr+dW_r and let P(t,x,t+1,¬∑) be its transition law over one unit of time. Let Q(1,x,¬∑) be the corresponding one-step transition law of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck equation dZ_r=AZ_rdr+dW_r, and set Œ≥:=Q(1,0,¬∑). For f_n(r,z):=‚ü®f(r,z),e_n‚ü© define Œ¥_n:=sup{|f_n(r,z)|:r‚â•0,z‚ààH}. Assume (A1) Œ¥_n<‚àû for all n and ‚àë_n Œ¥_nŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû; (A2) there exists Œ±>0 such that E exp{Œ±|f(s,Z^{t,x}(s))|^2}<‚àû for all t‚â•0, s‚àà[t,t+1], x‚ààH, where Z^{t,x} is the OU process started from x at t; and (A3) there exist Œ≤>1 and functions v_1,v_2:H‚ÜíR_+, bounded on bounded sets, such that E exp{Œ≤‚à´_t^{t+1}|f(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds}‚â§v_1(x)v_2(y) for every t‚â•0 and x,y‚ààH, where Zhat is the OU bridge from x at t to y at t+1. Let D:={y‚ààH:‚àë_n Œ¥_n|‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©|<‚àû}. Then for every bounded set B‚äÇH, P(t,x,t+1,¬∑) is absolutely continuous with respect to Q(1,x,¬∑) for every t‚â•0 and x‚ààH, and there exists a bounded measurable h:H‚ÜíR_+, depending on B, such that h(y)>0 for y‚ààD and inf{dP(t,x,t+1,¬∑)/dQ(1,x,¬∑)(y):x‚ààB,t‚â•0}‚â•h(y) for Œ≥-almost every y‚ààH.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The absolute continuity is obtained from Girsanov's theorem and the integrability hypotheses. Lemma 3.2 gives an explicit conditional density formula on D: dP/dQ(y)=E exp{œÜ(t,x,y)}, where œÜ is the L^1-limit of sums involving the drift coordinates along the pinned OU bridge. Lemma 3.1 supplies exponential estimates and uniform integrability for the terms in œÜ. In the final proof, for all m the stochastic-exponential part satisfies E|‚àë_{n‚â§m}‚à´ f_n d w_n‚àí(1/2)‚àë_{n‚â§m}‚à´|f_n|^2ds|‚â§2E exp{‚à´|f(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds}‚â§2v_1(x)v_2(y), using (A3). The deterministic endpoint and bridge-noise correction terms are controlled by Lemma 3.1(ii),(iii). For x in a fixed bounded B, v_1(x) is uniformly bounded, giving E|œÜ(t,x,y)|‚â§c_B[1+v_2(y)+‚àë_n(Œ¥_n|‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©|+Œ¥_ne^{‚àíŒª_n})], which is finite for y‚ààD. Jensen's inequality then yields E exp{œÜ(t,x,y)}‚â•exp{EœÜ(t,x,y)}‚â•exp{‚àíE|œÜ(t,x,y)|}. Defining h(y)=exp{‚àíg(y)} on D, with g the displayed upper bound, and h(y)=0 outside D, gives a bounded measurable lower bound that is strictly positive on D.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3 (uniform Cameron-Martin lower bound for OU kernels from bounded sets)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting where A is self-adjoint negative with eigenvalues ‚àíŒª_n and ‚àë_n Œª_n^{-1}<‚àû, let S(t) be the semigroup generated by A. Let Q(1,x,¬∑) be the law at time 1 of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process dZ_r=AZ_rdr+dW_r started from x, and let Œ≥:=Q(1,0,¬∑). For every bounded set B‚äÇH there exist constants Œ∫_1,Œ∫_2>0 such that dQ(1,x,¬∑)/dŒ≥(y)‚â•Œ∫_1 exp{‚àíŒ∫_2|y|} for every x‚ààB and Œ≥-almost every y‚ààH.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The OU laws are Gaussian: Q(1,x,¬∑)=N(S(1)x,Q_1) and Œ≥=N(0,Q_1), where Q_1=‚à´_0^1S(2r)dr and Q_1e_n=(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n})(2Œª_n)^{-1}e_n. Range(S(1)) is contained in Range(Q_1), and the closed graph theorem gives Q_1^{-1}S(1)‚ààL(H). The Cameron-Martin formula yields dQ(1,x,¬∑)/dŒ≥(y)=exp{‚ü®Q_1^{-1/2}S(1)x,Q_1^{-1/2}y‚ü©‚àí(1/2)|Q_1^{-1/2}S(1)x|^2}. This is bounded below by exp{‚àí|Q_1^{-1}S(1)x||y|‚àí(1/2)|Q_1^{-1/2}S(1)x|^2}. Since x ranges over a bounded set, the two coefficients are bounded uniformly, producing Œ∫_1 and Œ∫_2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4 (dissipativity gives moment bounds, tightness, and entrance into a ball)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X solve dX_t=[AX_t+f(t,X_t)]dt+dW_t in a separable Hilbert space H under the Section 2 assumptions on A and W. Assume f satisfies (A4): for every x‚ààH, t‚Ü¶f(t,x) is continuous, and for every r>0 there is K_r>0 such that |f(t,x)‚àíf(t,y)|‚â§K_r|x‚àíy| for t‚àà[0,r] and x,y in the open ball B_r={z‚ààH:|z|<r}. Assume also that there are measurable functions œâ:R_+‚ÜíR_+ and a:R_+^2‚ÜíR_+, with a bounded on bounded sets and a(t,¬∑) increasing, such that ‚ü®Ax+f(t,x+y),x‚ü©‚â§‚àíœâ(t)|x|^2+a(t,|y|)|x| for all t‚â•0, x‚ààD(A), y‚ààH; that inf_{œÉ‚â•0}‚à´_œÉ^{œÉ+T}œâ(Œª)dŒª‚Üí‚àû as T‚Üí‚àû; and that M:=sup_{t‚â•s‚â•0}‚à´_s^t exp{‚àí‚à´_r^tœâ(Œª)dŒª} E a(r,|œÜ(r)|)dr<‚àû, where œÜ(r):=‚à´_s^rS(r‚àíu)dW_u is the stochastic convolution. Put m:=sup_{t‚â•s‚â•0}E|‚à´_s^tS(t‚àír)dW_r|, which is finite under ‚àëŒª_n^{-1}<‚àû. Then (i) E|X^{s,x}(t)|‚â§m+|x|exp{‚àí‚à´_s^tœâ(Œª)dŒª}+M for every 0‚â§s‚â§t and x‚ààH. Moreover, (ii) for every Œµ>0 and R_1>0 there is R_0>0 such that P(œÉ,x,t,H‚àñB_{R_0})<Œµ for all x‚ààB_{R_1} and 0‚â§œÉ‚â§t, and (iii) for any beta_tilde,L>0 satisfying (1‚àíbeta_tilde)L>m+M and every R_2>0 there is T>0 such that P(œÉ,x,œÉ+t,B_L)‚â•beta_tilde for all x‚ààB_{R_2}, œÉ‚â•0, and t‚â•T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the mild solution as X(t)=v(t,s,x,œÜ)+œÜ(t), where œÜ is the stochastic convolution and v solves the deterministic random integral equation v(t)=S(t‚àís)x+‚à´_s^tS(t‚àír)f(r,v(r)+œà(r))dr for a fixed path œà. Approximate v by strong solutions v_n using the resolvent R(n)=n(nI‚àíA)^{-1}: v_n(t)=R(n)S(t‚àís)x+‚à´_s^tR(n)S(t‚àír)f(r,v(r)+œà(r))dr. Then v_n‚Üív uniformly on compact intervals and the residual Œ¥_n(t):=dv_n/dt‚àíAv_n‚àíf(t,v_n+œà) tends uniformly to zero. Differentiating |v_n(t)| and using the dissipativity inequality gives the left-derivative bound d^-|v_n(t)|/dt‚â§‚àíœâ(t)|v_n(t)|+a(t,|œà(t)|)+|Œ¥_n(t)|. Gronwall's inequality and the limit n‚Üí‚àû yield |v(t)|‚â§|x|e^{‚àí‚à´_s^tœâ}+‚à´_s^t e^{‚àí‚à´_r^tœâ}a(r,|œà(r)|)dr. Taking œà=œÜ and expectations gives (i), including the stochastic convolution contribution m. Chebyshev's inequality gives the tightness statement (ii). For (iii), choose T so that the exponentially damped initial term is uniformly small for |x|‚â§R_2, using inf_œÉ‚à´_œÉ^{œÉ+T}œâ‚Üí‚àû; then Chebyshev gives probability at least beta_tilde of being inside B_L.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.5 (linear growth with small slope implies the dissipativity hypotheses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert-space setting of Proposition 2.4, suppose f satisfies (A4) and the linear growth bound |f(t,x)|‚â§k_1+k_2|x| for all (t,x)‚ààR_+√óH, with k_1,k_2>0 and k_2<Œª_0, where Œª_0 is the lower bound in Ae_n=‚àíŒª_ne_n, Œª_n‚â•Œª_0. Then the dissipativity assumptions (2.16)-(2.18) of Proposition 2.4 hold, and therefore the moment, tightness, and ball-entrance conclusions of Proposition 2.4 hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since ‚ü®Ax,x‚ü©‚â§‚àíŒª_0|x|^2 for x‚ààD(A), the growth bound gives ‚ü®Ax+f(t,x+y),x‚ü©‚â§‚àíŒª_0|x|^2+(k_1+k_2|x+y|)|x|‚â§(‚àíŒª_0+k_2)|x|^2+(k_1+k_2|y|)|x|. Thus Proposition 2.4 applies with constant œâ=Œª_0‚àík_2>0 and a(t,Œ∏)=k_1+k_2Œ∏. The remaining integrability condition (2.18) follows from the finiteness of m and the exponential damping produced by the positive constant œâ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6 (main Hilbert-space l-condition, total variation convergence, and invariant measure uniqueness)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space, A self-adjoint negative with eigenbasis {e_n}, Ae_n=‚àíŒª_ne_n, Œª_n‚â•Œª_0>0, and ‚àëŒª_n^{-1}<‚àû, and W a cylindrical Wiener process. Let X solve dX_t=[AX_t+f(t,X_t)]dt+dW_t, and let P^*_{s,t} be the adjoint transition operators on probability measures on H. Define Œ¥_n:=sup{|‚ü®f(t,x),e_n‚ü©|:t‚â•0,x‚ààH}, Œ≥:=Q(1,0,¬∑) for the OU transition Q, and D:={y‚ààH:‚àëŒ¥_n|‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©|<‚àû}. Assume f satisfies (A1) Œ¥_n<‚àû and ‚àëŒ¥_nŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû; (A2) the one-point exponential integrability condition along OU paths; (A3) the bridge exponential integrability condition with some Œ≤>1 and v_1,v_2 bounded on bounded sets; (A4) local time-continuity and local Lipschitz continuity in x on bounded sets; the dissipativity and recurrence hypotheses (2.16)-(2.18) from Proposition 2.4; and Œ≥(D)>0. Then for all s‚â•0 and all probability measures ŒΩ_1,ŒΩ_2 on H, ‚à•P^*_{s,t}ŒΩ_1‚àíP^*_{s,t}ŒΩ_2‚à•‚Üí0 as t‚Üí‚àû. If f(t,x)=f(x) is autonomous, then the equation has a unique invariant probability measure Œº* and ‚à•P^*_tŒΩ‚àíŒº*‚à•‚Üí0 for every probability ŒΩ on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose L>m+M, where m and M are the constants in Proposition 2.4, and choose b>0 with (1‚àíb)L>m+M. Proposition 2.4(iii) gives the recurrence part of Proposition 1.7 with B(s)=B_L and Œ≤(s)=b, uniformly in s. For the one-step minorization, take x‚ààB_L and Œì‚ààB(H). Proposition 2.2 gives P(t,x,t+1,Œì)‚â•‚à´_Œìh(y)Q(1,x,dy), where h does not depend on x‚ààB_L or t and h>0 on D. Proposition 2.3 gives Q(1,x,dy)‚â•Œ∫_1e^{‚àíŒ∫_2|y|}Œ≥(dy) uniformly over x‚ààB_L. Hence P(t,x,t+1,Œì)‚â•Œºhat(Œì), where Œºhat(Œì):=Œ∫_1‚à´_Œìh(y)e^{‚àíŒ∫_2|y|}Œ≥(dy). Because Œ≥(D)>0 and h>0 on D, Œºhat(H)>0. Thus all assumptions of Proposition 1.7 are satisfied with a lower measure independent of s. Theorem 1.4 then gives total variation convergence of all evolved probabilities. In the autonomous case, Theorem 1.4(b) yields existence of an invariant probability measure, uniqueness, and convergence to it. The paper also notes that since D is a Borel linear subspace of H, the Kallianpur zero-one law implies Œ≥(D)=1 whenever Œ≥(D)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.7 (bounded drift version of Theorem 2.6)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting of Theorem 2.6, assume |f| is bounded, f satisfies (A4), and with Œ¥_n:=sup{|‚ü®f(t,x),e_n‚ü©|:t‚â•0,x‚ààH} one has ‚àëŒ¥_nŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû and Œ≥({y‚ààH:‚àëŒ¥_n|‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©|<‚àû})>0. Then all assumptions of Theorem 2.6 are satisfied, and hence its total variation convergence and autonomous invariant-measure conclusions hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The two displayed assumptions are exactly (A1) and Œ≥(D)>0. If |f| is bounded, then the OU-path and OU-bridge exponential integrability assumptions (A2) and (A3) are immediate. Boundedness also gives the linear-growth condition of Corollary 2.5 with k_2=0, so the dissipativity assumptions (2.16)-(2.18) hold. Therefore Theorem 2.6 applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.8 (three concrete sufficient conditions for the coordinate summability assumptions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting of Theorem 2.6, suppose f satisfies (A4) and |f| is bounded. Let Œ¥_n:=sup{|‚ü®f(t,x),e_n‚ü©|:t‚â•0,x‚ààH}. If at least one of the following holds, then the conclusions of Theorem 2.6 hold: (i) ‚àë_nŒ¥_n^2<‚àû; (ii) f maps R_+√óH boundedly into the fractional domain D_A^Œ± of (‚àíA)^Œ± for some Œ±>0 such that (‚àíA)^{‚àíŒ±} is Hilbert-Schmidt; or (iii) ‚àë_nŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 2.7 it suffices to verify ‚àëŒ¥_nŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû and Œ≥(D)>0. Under (i), Cauchy-Schwarz and ‚àëŒª_n^{-1}<‚àû give ‚àëŒ¥_nŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû, while ‚àëŒ¥_n|‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©|‚â§|y|(‚àëŒ¥_n^2)^{1/2}, so D=H and Œ≥(D)=1. Under (ii), ‚àëŒ¥_n^2‚â§sup_{t,x}|(‚àíA)^Œ±f(t,x)|^2‚àë_n|(‚àíA)^{‚àíŒ±}e_n|^2<‚àû, so (i) applies. Under (iii), boundedness of f gives sup_nŒ¥_n‚â§sup|f| and hence ‚àëŒ¥_nŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû. Moreover, for y‚ààD_A^{1/4}, ‚àëŒ¥_n|‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©|‚â§sup|f|(‚àëŒª_n^{-1/2})^{1/2}|(‚àíA)^{1/4}y|, so D contains D_A^{1/4}; the paper states that (iii) implies Œ≥(D_A^{1/4})=1, hence Œ≥(D)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 2.9 (quantitative choice of minorization time for Theorem 1.5)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Theorem 2.6, Proposition 2.4(i) and the lower measure Œºhat constructed in the proof of Theorem 2.6 can be combined with Theorem 1.5 to estimate convergence speed. The paper records the bound P(œÉ,x,t,H‚àñB_R)‚â§R^{-1}(m+|x|+M) for 0‚â§œÉ‚â§t, x‚ààH, R>0. Given ŒΩ_1,ŒΩ_2‚ààP and Œµ>0, it states that one can choose R>m+M such that P^*_{œÉ,t}ŒΩ_i(H‚àñB_R)<Œµ for i=1,2 and all 0‚â§œÉ‚â§t. For any probability Œº supported in B_R, œÉ‚â•0, œÑ‚â•1, and Œì‚ààB(H), P^*_{œÉ,œÉ+œÑ}Œº(Œì)‚â•inf{P(œÉ+œÑ‚àí1,x,œÉ+œÑ,Œì):x‚ààB_R} P^*_{œÉ,œÉ+œÑ‚àí1}Œº(B_R). Tracing Theorem 2.6 with B_R gives the first factor at least Œºhat(Œì), and choosing beta_tilde<1‚àíR^{-1}(m+M) gives P^*_{œÉ,œÉ+œÑ‚àí1}Œº(B_R)‚â•beta_tilde for œÑ=1+T, where T is chosen so that inf_{œÉ‚â•0}‚à´_œÉ^{œÉ+T}œâ(Œª)dŒª>‚àílog[1‚àíbeta_tilde‚àíR^{-1}(m+M)]. Thus ‚à•(P^*_{œÉ,œÉ+œÑ}Œº‚àíbeta_tilde Œºhat)^-‚à•=0 for all œÉ‚â•0 and all Œº supported in B_R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The estimate is obtained by inserting a last one-step transition into P^*_{œÉ,œÉ+œÑ}Œº and forcing the process to be in B_R at time œÉ+œÑ‚àí1. The last step is minorized uniformly by the measure Œºhat from the proof of Theorem 2.6. The probability of being in B_R at the penultimate time is bounded below using the moment estimate in Proposition 2.4(i) and Chebyshev's inequality, with T chosen large enough to damp the initial condition. This verifies condition (ii) of Theorem 1.5 with an explicit œÑ, while the first displayed tail estimate supplies condition (i).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (exponential estimates for terms in the pinned-OU density)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the Hilbert setting and hypotheses (A1)-(A3) of Proposition 2.2. Let f_n(s,x):=‚ü®f(s,x),e_n‚ü©. Fix t‚â•0, x,y‚ààH, and let Zhat be the OU bridge from x at t to y at t+1 with coordinates Zhat_n and Gaussian fluctuation terms Y_n. Define a_n(s,x_n,y_n):=2Œª_ne^{‚àíŒª_n(t+1‚àís)}(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n})^{-1}(y_n‚àíe^{‚àíŒª_n}x_n). Then: (i) there exist k>1 and c_k>0 such that for every m‚ààN and t‚â•0, E exp{k(‚àë_{n=1}^m‚à´_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))dw_n(s)‚àí(1/2)‚àë_{n=1}^m‚à´_t^{t+1}|f_n(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds)}‚â§c_kv_1(x)v_2(y). (ii) For every x‚ààH, y‚ààD, k>0, and m‚ààN, E exp{k|‚àë_{n=1}^m‚à´_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))a_n(s,x_n,y_n)ds|}‚â§exp{2k‚àë_{n=1}^‚àûŒ¥_n|y_n‚àíe^{‚àíŒª_n}x_n|/(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n})}<‚àû. (iii) For every k>0 there exists c'_k>0, depending only on k, such that E exp{k‚àë_{n=1}^m‚à´_t^{t+1}Œ¥_n[2Œª_ne^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)}/(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)})]|Y_n(s)|ds}‚â§c'_k for every m‚ààN and t‚â•0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says (i) follows from (A3) by the same argument as a standard exponential martingale estimate in Friedman, Stochastic Differential Equations and Applications, Chapter 7, Section 1. Part (ii) is a direct deterministic bound: use |f_n|‚â§Œ¥_n and integrate the explicit coefficient a_n over s to get an upper bound by 2‚àë_{n‚â§m}Œ¥_n|y_n‚àíe^{‚àíŒª_n}x_n|/(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n}); finiteness follows from y‚ààD and (A1). Part (iii) is stated to be completely analogous to Lemma 4.1 in Sim√£o [33], using the explicit Gaussian representation of Y_n and the summability in (A1) to obtain a bound uniform in m and t.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 (Girsanov formula conditioned on the OU endpoint)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the Hilbert setting and hypotheses (A1)-(A3) of Proposition 2.2. For t‚â•0, x‚ààH, and y‚ààD, define œÜ(t,x,y) as the L^1-limit as m‚Üí‚àû of ‚àë_{n=1}^mœÜ_n(t,x,y), where œÜ_n(t,x,y)=‚à´_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))dw_n(s)‚àí(1/2)‚à´_t^{t+1}|f_n(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds+‚à´_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))a_n(s,x_n,y_n)ds‚àí2‚à´_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))Œª_ne^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)}(1‚àíe^{‚àí2Œª_n(t+1‚àís)})^{-1}Y_n(s)ds. Here Zhat is the OU bridge from x at t to y at t+1, Y_n is its fluctuation term, and a_n is as in Lemma 3.1. Then for every t‚â•0 and x‚ààH, dP(t,x,t+1,¬∑)/dQ(1,x,¬∑)(y)=E exp{œÜ(t,x,y)} for Œ≥-almost every y‚ààD.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Girsanov's theorem gives the density of the nonlinear transition law with respect to the OU transition law as E(œÅ_t | Z^{t,x}(t+1)=y), where œÅ_t is the L^1-limit of finite-dimensional exponential martingales œÅ_t^m=exp{‚àë_{n=1}^m‚à´_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Z^{t,x}(s))dw_n(s)‚àí(1/2)‚àë_{n=1}^m‚à´_t^{t+1}|f_n(s,Z^{t,x}(s))|^2ds}. Conditional expectations of œÅ_t^m converge in L^1(Q(1,x,¬∑)) and along a subsequence almost everywhere; Q(1,x,¬∑) is equivalent to Œ≥ because the OU semigroup is strongly Feller in this setting. Conditioning the OU process on Z(t+1)=y replaces it by the pinned process Zhat. The stochastic differential equation for Zhat_n introduces extra drift terms; substituting it into the stochastic integral yields exactly the four terms œÜ_n. Lemma 3.1 gives uniform integrability of exp{‚àë_{n‚â§m}œÜ_n} and convergence to exp{œÜ}, so the finite-dimensional conditional density formula passes to the infinite-dimensional limit.

proof_techniques.name: Lower-measure l-condition for total variation merging
proof_techniques.description: The central abstract technique is to find a nonzero finite measure Œº_s such that, for every initial probability ŒΩ, the negative part of S_{s,t}ŒΩ‚àíŒº_s vanishes in total variation as t‚Üí‚àû. This says that all sufficiently late transition laws contain essentially the same common piece of mass. Once such a common piece exists uniformly with positive mass, the Lasota-Yorke argument forces the total variation distance between any two evolved probability measures to go to zero. In the homogeneous case this yields a unique attracting invariant probability measure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, it would be enough to prove an analogue on X=C_0([0,1]): every pair of transition laws eventually shares a common nonzero submeasure. This can bypass a direct strong Feller proof for the singular Œ¥0 drift.

proof_techniques.name: Recurrence plus local one-step minorization
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 1.7 decomposes the l-condition into two verifiable ingredients: a recurrence or entrance estimate showing that from any starting point the process reaches a fixed set B with probability at least Œ≤ at all sufficiently large times, and a uniform one-step minorization P(t,x,t+1,¬∑)‚â•Œºhat(¬∑) for all x‚ààB and all relevant t. The product Œ≤ Œºhat is then a lower measure. This is a Harris-type small set argument formulated in the language of lower measures and total variation convergence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Œ¥0 SPDE, one can try to show that solutions enter a bounded subset of C_0([0,1]) or an L^2-ball with positive probability, and that from that bounded set the one-step transition kernels dominate a common measure. If successful, uniqueness of invariant measures follows immediately from Theorem 1.4.

proof_techniques.name: Girsanov comparison with a Gaussian reference process
proof_techniques.description: For additive-noise semilinear equations, the paper compares the nonlinear transition law P(t,x,t+1,¬∑) to the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck transition law Q(1,x,¬∑). Girsanov's theorem expresses the Radon-Nikodym derivative as the conditional expectation of an exponential martingale given the endpoint of the OU process. Conditioning on the endpoint turns the OU path into a pinned OU bridge; the bridge representation allows the density to be written as E exp{œÜ(t,x,y)}. Exponential estimates and Jensen's inequality then produce a strictly positive lower bound on this density on a Gaussian-positive set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For regularized drifts G_{1/n}(u), this suggests proving uniform-in-n lower bounds of transition densities relative to the stochastic heat equation without drift, then passing to the Œ¥0 limit if the convergence of transition laws is strong enough on suitable test sets. The singular drift makes direct Girsanov delicate, but the approximation framework in the problem may make this route viable.

proof_techniques.name: Pinned Ornstein-Uhlenbeck bridge calculations
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses an explicit coordinate formula for the OU bridge between endpoints x and y over a unit time interval. This formula separates deterministic endpoint interpolation terms from Gaussian fluctuation terms Y_n. In the density proof, the bridge stochastic differential equation produces endpoint correction terms and singular-looking coefficients near the terminal time; Lemma 3.1 controls these terms using coordinate summability of the drift and Gaussian estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the one-dimensional Dirichlet stochastic heat equation, the OU bridge of the linear stochastic heat equation has an analogous spectral representation using sine eigenfunctions. This could be used to analyze endpoint-conditioned versions of approximating equations and to build a common lower bound for transition probabilities.

proof_techniques.name: Uniform Cameron-Martin lower bound from bounded initial sets
proof_techniques.description: After obtaining a lower bound for dP/dQ, the paper still needs a common reference measure independent of the initial point x in a bounded set. Proposition 2.3 supplies this by comparing Q(1,x,¬∑) to Œ≥=Q(1,0,¬∑). The Cameron-Martin formula shows that the Gaussian density is bounded below by Œ∫_1e^{‚àíŒ∫_2|y|} uniformly over x in a bounded set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirichlet heat semigroup on [0,1], the same Gaussian-shift comparison should be available in an L^2 formulation. It can convert a lower bound relative to the linear transition from u_0 into a lower bound relative to a fixed Gaussian law.

proof_techniques.name: Dissipativity and stochastic convolution estimates for recurrence
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.4 proves recurrence estimates by writing the solution as a stochastic convolution plus a deterministic equation driven by the convolution. A dissipativity inequality for ‚ü®Ax+f(t,x+y),x‚ü© gives a Gronwall bound on the deterministic part, while the stochastic convolution has uniformly bounded first moment because A^{-1} is nuclear. Chebyshev's inequality then gives boundedness in probability and positive-probability entrance into a fixed ball.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, the heat operator with Dirichlet boundary is dissipative and the drift Œ¥0 is nonnegative but singular. One possible task is to derive an analogue of the moment or Lyapunov estimate for the ABLM24 mild solution, perhaps first for G_{1/n} approximations and then in the limit.

proof_techniques.name: Quantitative convergence by local l-condition
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 1.5 refines the lower-measure method by allowing a small tail error Œµ outside a set B and a local lower-measure condition only for measures supported in B. The proof normalizes restrictions to B, contracts their signed difference using the common lower measure, and carries the tail error through an induction. This gives an explicit geometric term plus an Œµ-dependent residual.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can estimate the probability that the Œ¥0 SPDE lies outside a good bounded set and obtain a local minorization time œÑ, Theorem 1.5 gives not only uniqueness but a route to quantitative mixing estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-dimensional truncation and uniform integrability of exponential martingales
proof_techniques.description: The infinite-dimensional Girsanov density is obtained by truncating to the first m eigen-directions, proving estimates uniformly in m, and passing to an L^1 limit. Lemma 3.1 provides uniform exponential bounds for the stochastic exponential and the pinned-bridge correction terms, ensuring uniform integrability. This avoids formal manipulation of infinite sums of stochastic integrals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For approximating the singular drift, a similar spectral truncation strategy may help justify density comparisons or lower bounds at finite dimension before taking limits in both truncation and drift regularization.

other_useful_info: Remark 2.1 shows that assumption (A1) can be quite restrictive for Nemytskii drifts f(x)(Œæ)=F(x(Œæ)) in H=L^2(D): under a mild condition on a test function g, (A1) forces F to be bounded. This warns that the Hilbert-space theorem does not automatically cover rough pointwise drifts, and certainly not the distributional Œ¥0 drift, without additional approximation or smoothing arguments. The paper notes that D={y:‚àëŒ¥_n|‚ü®y,e_n‚ü©|<‚àû} is a Borel linear subspace, so Œ≥(D)>0 implies Œ≥(D)=1 by Kallianpur's zero-one law. The examples indicate where the Section 2 theorem is naturally applicable: fourth-order parabolic equations with bounded Nemytskii drift work because Œª_n grows like n^4 and ‚àëŒª_n^{-1/2}<‚àû, while second-order heat equations require smoothing in the drift, such as an integral kernel mapping into H^1, to satisfy the coordinate summability assumptions. The paper cites Da Prato-Elworthy-Zabczyk, Maslowski, Chojnowska-Michalik-Goldys, Peszat-Zabczyk, and others for strong Feller and irreducibility approaches, but its lower-measure method is presented as an alternative route to ergodicity and mixing.
why_relevant: The lower-measure method gives an alternative route to uniqueness and mixing of invariant measures for stochastic semilinear equations. This is useful if the Œ¥0 drift obstructs a clean strong Feller proof.
